questions.stem: What is the defining structural property of a singly linked list versus an array?
options: Nodes store a value and a next pointer; elements need not be contiguous in memory
All nodes live in one contiguous block and support O(1) index access
Each node has both prev and next pointers by default
Insertion always requires shifting all later elements
questions.explanation: Singly linked lists chain nodes via next pointers; memory need not be contiguous, so index access is O(n).

questions.stem: In a doubly linked list, what extra information does each node typically hold compared to a singly linked list?
options: A hash of the payload for O(1) lookup
A pointer to the previous node in addition to next
An array of child pointers
Only a previous pointer; next is implied
questions.explanation: Doubly linked nodes carry both prev and next, enabling O(1) backward traversal and easier deletion when you hold the node.

questions.stem: In a circular singly linked list with at least one node, what does the last node's next pointer reference?
options: null / nullptr
The second node
The head (first) node
Itself only when the list has size 1; otherwise null
questions.explanation: Circular lists close the ring: the last node's next points back to the head (and a one-node circular list points to itself).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why do many list algorithms introduce a dummy (sentinel) head node? Which response is most accurate?
options: To force the list into contiguous memory
To make random access O(1)
To store the list length in O(1) without counting
To avoid special-casing empty lists and head updates during insert/delete
questions.explanation: A dummy head unifies edge cases: operations always mutate next of some node, so head insertion/deletion needs no separate branch.

questions.stem: Given only the head pointer of a singly linked list, what is the typical time complexity to access the k-th node (0-indexed)?
options: O(k), which is O(n) in the worst case
Option claiming: O(1)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
questions.explanation: You must walk k next pointers from the head; there is no index arithmetic like arrays.

questions.stem: Inserting a new node after a given node pointer in a singly linked list is O(1) because:
options: The CPU can index the node in constant time from an array offset
You rewrite two pointers (new.next and node.next) without shifting other nodes
Garbage collection automatically relocates neighbors
You must still scan from head, so it is actually O(n)
questions.explanation: With a direct pointer to the predecessor, pointer rewiring is constant time; finding that predecessor from head alone is still O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Compared to dynamic arrays, linked lists typically suffer more from:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Inability to grow beyond a fixed compile-time capacity
Mandatory O(n) insert at the end when you already hold a tail pointer
Poor cache locality and pointer-chasing overhead due to scattered node allocations
Inability to store heterogeneous value types
questions.explanation: Nodes are often heap-allocated separately, so traversal jumps around memory (fragmentation / cache misses) versus contiguous arrays.

questions.stem: To delete a node in a singly linked list when you only have a pointer to that node (not its predecessor), which constraint usually applies?
options: Always impossible in a singly linked list
You free the node and the previous next updates automatically
You set the node's next to null and the list heals
You can copy the next node's value into this node and unlink the next (fails for the true tail)
questions.explanation: Classic trick: overwrite with successor's data then bypass the successor. It does not work cleanly for the last node.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: What does reversing a singly linked list in place primarily rearrange? Which answer is best?
options: The direction of next pointers so the former head becomes the tail
Node values into sorted order
Memory so all nodes become contiguous
Prev pointers only; next stays the same
questions.explanation: In-place reverse walks the list once, flipping next links (prev/curr/next pattern) so order of traversal reverses.

questions.stem: If you already hold a pointer to the tail of a singly linked list, appending a new node is:
options: O(n) because you must start from head
O(1) by setting tail.next and updating the tail reference
O(log n) with binary search for the end
Amortized O(1) only if the list is circular
questions.explanation: A maintained tail pointer makes append constant time; without it, finding the end is O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which statement about memory for arrays vs linked lists is most accurate? Which answer is best?
options: Arrays always use more memory than linked lists of the same length
Linked lists never fragment the heap
Linked lists pay per-node pointer overhead; arrays pay for unused capacity when over-allocated
Arrays cannot store pointers to other structures
questions.explanation: Lists add pointer fields (and allocator metadata); dynamic arrays may reserve extra slots. Trade-offs depend on usage.

questions.stem: In interview code, checking for an empty singly linked list usually means:
options: head.next == null always
length field equals −1
tail points to head
head == null
questions.explanation: An empty list is represented by a null head (unless a sentinel is used; then sentinel.next == null).

questions.stem: Why is finding the middle of a singly linked list commonly done with two pointers rather than counting length first?
options: Slow/fast (tortoise/hare) finds the middle in one pass without a prior length scan
Two pointers run in O(1) total time
Counting length is impossible on linked lists
It avoids using any extra variables
questions.explanation: Advance fast by two and slow by one; when fast finishes, slow is at the middle—single O(n) pass, O(1) space.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: A key advantage of a doubly linked list for LRU-cache style designs is:? Which answer is best?
options: O(1) index access to the k-th entry
O(1) removal of a node when you already hold its pointer (rewire prev/next)
Automatic sorting of keys
Half the memory of a singly linked list
questions.explanation: With a node handle, doubly linked lists splice out in O(1); singly lists need the predecessor.

questions.stem: What happens if you lose the head pointer of a singly linked list and have no other references into the chain?
options: You can recover the head by walking backward from any node
The OS automatically rebuilds the head from the heap
The nodes become unreachable (leaked until GC/manual free) and the list is effectively gone
Tail uniquely determines the head
questions.explanation: Singly linked nodes do not point backward; without an entry pointer, the chain is unreachable.

questions.stem: Inserting at the front of a singly linked list (updating head) is typically:
options: Option claiming: O(n)
O(log n)
Option claiming: O(n²)
Option claiming: O(1)
questions.explanation: New node points to old head; head is reassigned—constant pointer work.

questions.stem: Which best describes a circular doubly linked list often used with a sentinel?
options: Both next and prev form rings, and a sentinel can sit between head and tail logically
Only next pointers form a circle; prev is always null
It is identical to a binary tree with parent links
It forbids deletion of any node
questions.explanation: Sentinel-based circular DLL designs make empty/non-empty operations uniform via prev/next of the sentinel.

questions.stem: When comparing two singly linked lists for equality of sequence values, the safe approach is:
options: Compare only the head pointers for identity
Walk both lists in lockstep comparing values until both end
Hash only the first node of each list
Compare lengths only
questions.explanation: Structural equality of sequences requires pairwise value comparison (and matching lengths).

questions.stem: Why might an interviewer ask you to implement a list with a dummy head even if the problem statement does not require it?
options: It guarantees O(1) random access
It is required by the language runtime
It simplifies boundary handling and reduces off-by-one / null bugs in merge and remove code
It halves asymptotic time complexity
questions.explanation: Dummy heads are a robustness technique for cleaner pointer logic, not an asymptotic shortcut.

questions.stem: Space overhead of a singly linked list of n integer nodes (ignoring allocator padding) is dominated by:
options: n integers only, same as an array
A single pointer regardless of n
n² pointers for full connectivity
n integers plus n next pointers (plus possible object headers)
questions.explanation: Each node stores payload and a next reference; that pointer cost is the classic list vs array trade-off.

questions.stem: Floyd’s cycle detection uses a slow and fast pointer. If a cycle exists, what eventually happens?
options: Slow and fast meet inside the cycle
Fast reaches null before slow moves
Slow always overtakes fast from behind in one step
Both pointers stay at the head forever
questions.explanation: In a cycle, fast gains on slow and they collide; if there is no cycle, fast hits null.

questions.stem: After detecting a cycle with Floyd, how do you find the cycle’s entrance (start of the loop)?
options: Return the meeting point; it is always the entrance
Reset one pointer to head and advance both one step at a time until they meet
Binary search on node addresses
Reverse the list and take the new head
questions.explanation: Classic second phase: one pointer at head, one at meeting point; same-speed walk meets at the cycle start.

questions.stem: Merging two sorted singly linked lists into one sorted list is typically done by:
options: Concatenating then sorting with quicksort on values only
Hashing all values into a set
Walking both lists and splicing the smaller head each time (often with a dummy)
Converting both to arrays, then linking randomly
questions.explanation: Two-pointer merge reuses existing nodes in O(n+m) time and O(1) extra space (iterative).

questions.stem: To reverse nodes in groups of k (e.g., 1→2→3→4 with k=2 → 2→1→4→3), a solid approach is:
options: Reverse the entire list once
Use a stack of size n always, ignoring k
Swap only adjacent pairs regardless of k
Count k nodes, reverse that segment, connect to previous and recurse/iterate on the rest
questions.explanation: Process the list in k-sized windows: reverse each full window and link windows carefully; leftover <k may stay.

questions.stem: Finding whether two singly linked lists intersect (share a suffix by reference) can be done by:
options: Aligning starts after length difference, then walking together until a shared node or null
Comparing only lengths; equal length means they intersect
Sorting both lists by value
Checking if heads have equal values
questions.explanation: Intersection by identity: equalize remaining length, then advance; first common node is the join (or null).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Removing the n-th node from the end in one pass is commonly done by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Counting length, then making a second full pass always
Leading pointer n+1 steps ahead of a trailing pointer (with dummy), then deleting trailing.next
Using a max-heap of node indices
Reversing twice and deleting the n-th from the new head only
questions.explanation: Two pointers with gap n maintain the delete position; dummy handles deleting the original head.

questions.stem: Why is “insert in O(1)” incomplete if you only say “linked list insert is O(1)”?
options: Insert is never O(1) on linked lists
O(1) requires a doubly linked list only
O(1) holds when you already have a pointer to the insert location (or predecessor); finding that spot from head is O(n)
O(1) requires the list to be circular
questions.explanation: Pointer rewiring is O(1); locating where to insert from the head is search cost.

questions.stem: Palindrome check on a singly linked list in O(n) time and O(1) extra space typically:
options: Converts to a string and uses two indices
Uses recursion with O(n) call-stack and claims O(1) space
Sorts node values in place
Finds the middle, reverses the second half, compares, then optionally restores
questions.explanation: Middle + reverse second half + compare is the classic O(1)-extra-space approach (careful with odd length).

questions.stem: Adding two numbers stored as reverse-digit linked lists (least significant digit at head) is similar to:
options: Schoolbook addition with a carry while both lists (and carry) remain
DFS on a tree
Merge sort’s combine step only
Binary search on digit values
questions.explanation: Walk both lists, sum digits + carry, emit new nodes—same idea as adding integers digit-wise.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Detecting a cycle using a hash set of visited node identities uses:? Choose the strongest option.
options: O(1) extra space always
O(n) extra space in the worst case, O(n) time
O(log n) space via binary lifting
No extra space and no pointer mutation
questions.explanation: Storing seen nodes trades space for a simple cycle check; Floyd uses O(1) space instead.

questions.stem: When splitting a list into two halves for merge sort on linked lists, why prefer slow/fast over converting to an array?
options: Arrays cannot hold object references
Merge sort is illegal on arrays
You can split in O(n) with O(1) extra space and sort by pointer splicing without random access
Slow/fast runs in O(log n)
questions.explanation: Linked-list merge sort finds mid, sorts halves, merges—natural fit without needing index access.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Reordering list L0→L1→…→Ln-1→Ln to L0→Ln→L1→Ln-1→… often uses:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Only a stack of all nodes, never finding the middle
Random shuffle of pointers
Sort by value then reverse
Find middle, reverse second half, weave the two halves together
questions.explanation: Standard pattern: mid split, reverse back half, merge by alternating nodes.

questions.stem: Copying a linked list where each node has next and a random pointer (deep copy) is often solved by:
options: Hash map old→new nodes, or interleaving cloned nodes then restoring
Shallow copying only next
Sorting by random pointer addresses
Ignoring random and hoping tests miss it
questions.explanation: Map or weave techniques ensure random links point to clones, not originals.

questions.stem: In a problem “swap every two adjacent nodes,” what should you be careful about?
options: Only swapping values, never pointers, is required by all interviewers
Updating head when the first pair swaps, and linking pairs without losing the rest of the list
Using recursion that always uses O(1) stack
Ensuring the list becomes circular afterward
questions.explanation: Pointer swaps change the head for the first pair; keep references to the next pair before rewiring.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Removing duplicates from a sorted singly linked list is easiest because:? Which answer is best?
options: You need a global hash set even when sorted
Sorting again inside the algorithm is mandatory
Duplicates are adjacent, so one pass comparing curr and curr.next suffices
You must reverse first
questions.explanation: Sorted order colocates duplicates; skip equal neighbors. Unsorted needs a set or sort first.

questions.stem: Why can merge sort be preferred over quicksort for linked lists in interviews?
options: Quicksort cannot partition without arrays
Merge sort uses less than O(n log n) comparisons on lists
Quicksort is unstable only on lists
Merge is natural with pointer splicing; random-access pivots/partitions are awkward on lists
questions.explanation: Lists lack cheap indexing; merging by pointers fits; efficient in-place quicksort-style partition is harder.

questions.stem: Given a node to delete in a doubly linked list (not the only node), correct unlinking includes:
options: node.prev.next = node.next and node.next.prev = node.prev (with null checks at ends)
Only setting node.next = null
Freeing without updating neighbors
Setting only node.prev = null
questions.explanation: Both neighboring links must bypass the deleted node; handle head/tail when prev or next is null.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Rotating a list right by k places efficiently usually:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Performs k single-node moves from head each time in O(nk)
Finds length, reduces k mod length, finds new tail/head cut point, and relinks once
Uses a queue of size k²
Sorts then rotates
questions.explanation: One length pass, modular k, one cut-and-relink yields O(n) time.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When would you choose a linked list over a dynamic array in production? Which answer is best?
options: You need the fastest possible scans of large numeric buffers
You need minimal pointer overhead and great locality
You need frequent middle inserts/deletes given node handles, and random access is rare
You need O(1) index reads as the primary operation
questions.explanation: Lists shine for splice-heavy workloads with known positions; arrays win on locality and indexing.

questions.stem: A “zigzag” or odd-even list rearrangement (odds then evens by position) should:
options: Sort values into odd/even numeric groups
Option claiming: Use BFS
Require a doubly linked list
Split by index parity via two pointer chains, then connect odd tail to even head
questions.explanation: Maintain odd and even lists by position, preserve relative order, then concatenate.

questions.stem: Senior pitfall: your cycle detector “works” on interviews but mutates next pointers to mark visited. What risk do you introduce?
options: You destroy the list structure; concurrent readers or later reuse see corruption unless you restore perfectly
None—mutation is always fine
You only risk integer overflow
You improve cache locality permanently
questions.explanation: Destructive marking is a side-effect hazard; Floyd or a set avoids permanently breaking shared lists.

questions.stem: You implement delete(node) by copying next’s value, but the interviewer asks about deleting the tail. What is the honest limitation?
options: It works for the tail if you set next to null
Without the predecessor (or a doubly linked prev), you cannot unlink the true tail this way
Tails are never deleted in singly linked lists
Copying always allocates a new tail automatically
questions.explanation: The copy-trick needs a successor; the last node has none, so you need prev or a full scan from head.

questions.stem: In a concurrent environment, why is a naive lock-free singly linked list push still subtle?
options: Linked lists cannot be concurrent by definition
Locks are illegal in all languages
CAS on head can suffer ABA; memory reclamation (hazard pointers/RCU/GC) must be correct
FIFO ordering appears automatically without care
questions.explanation: ABA and safe reclamation dominate real lock-free list design beyond a textbook CAS loop.

questions.stem: Memory fragmentation concern: frequent allocate/free of tiny list nodes can cause:
options: Only faster sequential scans
The OS to convert the list into an array automatically
O(1) guarantees for all operations
Allocator pressure and poor locality; pooling/arena allocation is a common mitigation
questions.explanation: Node-churn fragments heaps and hurts caches; custom allocators or arrays-of-nodes are practical fixes.

questions.stem: Intersection of lists by value equality vs by node reference—why do interviews emphasize reference?
options: Y-shaped intersection problems mean shared suffix nodes (same objects), not merely equal payloads
Values are never comparable
Reference equality is slower to test
Value equality always implies shared memory
questions.explanation: Classic “intersection” is structural sharing; equal numbers in separate nodes do not count.

questions.stem: After reversing the second half for palindrome check, a production-quality solution often:
options: Leaves the list permanently reversed as a feature
Restores the list (re-reverse) if callers expect the original order
Converts to an array and discards the list
Deletes the second half
questions.explanation: Interview demos may skip restore; real APIs should not mutate observably unless documented.

questions.stem: Off-by-one with dummy heads: deleting the n-th from end fails when gap is wrong. What usually went wrong?
options: Dummy heads cannot be used for this problem
correctIndex arithmetic in MCQs
Fast advanced n steps instead of n+1 (or vice versa) relative to where dummy sits
Integer overflow of n alone
questions.explanation: The lead distance must account for whether trailing starts at dummy or head—classic fencepost bug.

questions.stem: Why might an intrusive linked list (node embedded in objects) be used in kernels/games?
options: It forces GC pauses
It makes algorithms asymptotically faster than O(n)
It removes the need for prev/next entirely
Zero extra heap node allocation, better locality/control, objects can sit on multiple lists carefully
questions.explanation: Intrusive lists avoid separate node objects and give precise lifetime/layout control.

questions.stem: You claim “linked list insert is always better than ArrayList.” A senior rebuttal is:
options: ArrayList insert at end is amortized O(1) with far better constants/locality; middle insert still needs O(n) find on a list
Agree—lists always dominate
ArrayList cannot insert at all
Big-O ignores constants so lists win every benchmark
questions.explanation: Real performance cares about locality and whether you already have the insert position; lists are not free wins.

questions.stem: Circular list + flawed termination (while curr != null) causes what failure mode?
options: Immediate null pointer on first node
Infinite loop because null is never reached
Automatic conversion to a doubly linked list
Guaranteed stack overflow only
questions.explanation: Circular structures never hit null; terminate on returning to start or use a visited set / cycle-aware walk.
